w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: CAP Theorem — AP vs CP Systems
Take-Home Assignment | Go Language | Docker Desktop
Two distributed key-value stores: one AP, one CP
Your own laptop — Docker Desktop required
4–5 hours including experiments and discussion questions
Not required — this assignment is fully independent
Brewer's CAP theorem states that a distributed system can guarantee at most 2 of these 3 properties simultaneously:
All nodes return the same data at the same time
Refuses a write rather than risk nodes having different values
Every request always gets a response
Always accepts reads and writes, even if data may be temporarily stale
System works even if nodes cannot communicate
Non-negotiable in real networks — packets do get lost
Because partitions WILL happen in any real network, P is not optional. The real design choice is: when a partition occurs, do you stay Consistent (CP) or Available (AP)? CP: refuse
rather than risk inconsistency. AP: always respond, accept that some reads may be slightly stale.
Always accepts reads and writes. Nodes sync every second. May briefly return stale values.
DNS, shopping carts, social media likes
Requires 3 out of 5 nodes to agree. Refuses
if majority unreachable. Reads always consistent.
Complete all setup steps before touching any code. If Docker is not installed and running, nothing else will work.
Docker Desktop runs containers on your laptop. It is free for students.
Requires WSL2 — installer will guide you
Or install Docker Engine + Compose plugin
After installing, open Docker Desktop and wait for it to show the green running status. Then verify in a terminal:
# Should show: Docker version 24.x.x or higher
# Should show: Docker Compose version 2.x.x or higher
# On newer Docker: try 'docker compose version' (no hyphen)
Download lab03-complete.zip from Moodle and unzip it:
This compiles the skeleton code and creates a Docker image called lab03. Run from inside the lab03-complete folder:
# Takes 2-3 minutes on first run (downloads Go compiler)
# Expected last line: Successfully tagged lab03:latest
Your environment is ready. Move on to Part 1. The pre-compiled binaries run the skeleton code — put and get commands connect but do nothing useful until you implement the TODO functions.
Check Docker Desktop is running (green icon in taskbar/menu bar). Then try: docker-compose -f docker/docker-
restart. If still failing, check: docker logs lab03-ap-node1 for the error.
How to Recompile After Editing Code
Every time you edit a source file you must recompile and restart. Do this inside the container:
You must recompile AND restart in EVERY container that runs that code. If you fix
in ap-node1, you need to recompile and restart in ap-node2, ap-node3, ap-node4, ap-node5 as well — all 5 containers share the same code.
# CLI shortcut — use from your laptop terminal (not inside container)
Open the AP source files for editing. All files are inside the ap-node1 container at /lab03/ap/
used as version number for conflict resolution
Store key locally with current timestamp — no coordination, always succeeds
Return local value immediately — may be stale, that is correct for AP
Update store keeping higher timestamp per key — last-write-wins
Send local store snapshot to one peer via Sync RPC
() for every peer on every ticker tick
Each handler calls the correct local function and fills the reply struct
# Get from node 5 immediately — may be stale
# May return: not found (sync not happened yet — this is correct)
# Wait 3 seconds then get from node 5 again
() as a version number (Week 10: Time and
will cover Vector Clocks — a proper solution). We broadcast to ALL peers every second (Week 6: Message-oriented communication will cover Gossip protocols — a more efficient approach).
— return true if reachable, false if error
Send Write RPC to all peers concurrently using goroutines and a buffered channel
Send Read RPC to all peers concurrently, collect Entry responses
Write/Read store locally. Put/Get call quorum functions and set
With 5 nodes (self + 4 peers), quorum = 5/2 + 1 = 3. This means:
self (always counts) + 1 peer = 2 FAIL — quorum not reached
picks the one with the highest timestamp (most recent write)
# Normal put and get — all 5 nodes up
# Stop 3 nodes and try to write
# Expected: quorum not reached: got 2 need 3
We use simple majority counting here (Week 9: Consensus will cover Raft and Paxos — proper algorithms that survive node crashes, not just partitions). We use
Part 3 — Experiments: AP vs CP Comparison
Both systems must be fully working (all 15 tasks implemented and compiled). Restart all containers first to get a clean state: docker-compose -f docker/docker-
Verify all 10 containers are running: docker
AP: put 5 different keys from ap-node1, get all 5 from ap-node5 after 3 seconds
CP: put 5 different keys from cp-node1, get all 5 from cp-node5
AP: did all 5 gets succeed after 3 seconds?
Experiment B — Stale Read — The Core AP vs CP Difference
Immediately (within 1 second) get 'version' from ap-node5 — record result
Wait 3 seconds, get 'version' from ap-node5 again — record result
Immediately get 'version' from cp-node5 — record result
did ap-node5 return immediately? What after 3 seconds?
CP: what did cp-node5 return immediately?
you a stale read and why is this acceptable for AP?
Experiment C — Minority Partition — 2 Nodes Down
Stop 2 AP nodes: docker stop lab03-ap-node4 lab03-ap-node5
AP: put key 'status'='active' from ap-node1 — does it succeed?
AP: get 'status' from ap-node1, ap-node2, ap-node3
Wait 5 seconds — get 'status' from all 5 AP nodes
Repeat steps 1-3 for CP (stop cp-node4, cp-node5)
Did AP accept the write with only 3 nodes? (3 = quorum for 5 nodes)
Did CP accept the write with only 3 nodes?
After restart, did all AP nodes eventually
Experiment D — Majority Partition — 3 Nodes Down
Stop 3 AP nodes: docker stop lab03-ap-node3 lab03-ap-node4 lab03-ap-node5
AP: get 'alert' from ap-node1 and ap-node2
Restart: docker start lab03-ap-node3 lab03-ap-node4 lab03-ap-node5
Wait 5 seconds — get 'alert' from all 5 AP nodes
Repeat for CP (stop cp-node3, cp-node4, cp-node5)
After AP recovery: are all 5 nodes consistent? How long did it take?
Experiment E — Conflicting Writes — AP Only
Block ap-node3 outgoing traffic: docker exec lab03-ap-node3 iptables -A OUTPUT -j DROP
Write 'score'='999' from ap-node3 (it cannot reach others)
Wait 5 seconds — get 'score' from all 5 AP nodes
What value does each node return for 'score' after recovery?
Which value won the conflict? Why? (hint: look at timestamps)
Note: CP cannot have conflicting writes by design — skip CP for this experiment
Answer all four questions. Submit answers via the Moodle quiz. You may also write answers here for your own reference.
In Experiment B, the AP system
a stale value immediately after a write. How many seconds did it take to converge to the correct value? Which line of code in
controls this? What would happen if you changed it to 10 seconds?
In Experiment D, the CP system refused writes when 3 nodes were down. The quorum formula is
(peers)/2 + 1 = 3. Why is this threshold 3 and not 2? What problem would occur if you changed the quorum to 2?

() as a version number for conflict resolution in the AP system. Describe a realistic scenario where this approach gives the wrong result. How would vector clocks (covered in Week 10) solve this problem?
A hospital patient record system vs a Twitter like counter — which should be CP and which AP? Use specific results from your Experiments B and D to justify your answer.
Works when majority of nodes down
AP Tasks 1-8 all implemented and tested
AP: put from node1, get from node5 after 3s returns correct value
CP Tasks 9-15 all implemented and tested
CP: write fails with error when 3 nodes are down
Experiments A-E completed for both systems
AP vs CP comparison table filled in
All 4 discussion questions answered on Moodle
Install Docker Desktop from docker.com — make sure it is running
Try: docker compose (no hyphen) — newer Docker uses this
Run from inside lab03-complete folder, not a subfolder
Run: docker-compose down then docker-compose up -d
You returned nil in Task 1 — return a &Node{...} pointer
Run iptables inside the container, not on your laptop
Run: docker-compose down, then up again
// Send RPC to N peers concurrently, collect results
// pass peer as argument — avoid loop variable bug
// Collect ALL results (must read from channel for every goroutine launched)
// NEVER call RPC while holding a lock — can deadlock
